skill.name: claude-hooks
skill.description: Guides creation of Claude Code hooks for tool validation, audit logging, notifications, and workflow automation.
skill.summary: Create Claude Code hook configurations for validation, logging, alerts, and automation.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: The static findings are mostly markdown false positives, including weak cryptography and system reconnaissance alerts with no supporting evidence. The real risk is that the skill documents Claude Code hooks that execute shell commands and can write files under the user home directory. No prompt injection, network exfiltration, or malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Shell Command Hooks
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The reference describes hook entries with type command and command strings. Claude Code hooks can run automatically when matching events occur, so unsafe commands could affect the local system.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command fields are explicit in the reference examples, and the skill purpose is hook automation. The examples are simple, so this is a capability risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.
medium_findings.remediation: Warn users to review every hook command, keep matchers narrow, and avoid commands that process untrusted input without quoting.

medium_findings.title: Hook Example Writes To Hidden Claude Directory
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The logging example appends file path data to ~/.claude/file_log.txt. This is a local audit log pattern, but it writes to a hidden home directory file during hook execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden directory path and append redirection are directly present. It appears intended for logging, not stealth or persistence.
medium_findings.remediation: Document where logs are written and recommend user-controlled paths, rotation, and exclusion of sensitive values.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Findings Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static analysis reported Ruby shell backtick execution on markdown tables and code fences. These lines are documentation syntax, not executable Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown inline code or fenced JSON examples. No Ruby execution context exists in the scanned files.
low_findings.remediation: No remediation required for markdown formatting.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography, but the cited lines contain a description line and an environment variable table header. No cryptographic API or hash use was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact cited lines do not contain cryptographic code or algorithm names. They are frontmatter text and table formatting.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static analysis flagged system reconnaissance, but the cited lines discuss logging volume and output verbosity. No system inventory command or data collection pattern was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are best-practice guidance about concise output. They do not invoke host discovery tools or collect system details.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands In Hook Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference shows hook command fields that can execute shell commands when Claude Code events match. This is powerful and should be reviewed before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hook command examples are explicit and match the skill purpose. The documented examples are not malicious by themselves.
dangerous_patterns.remediation: Require manual review of generated hook commands before adding them to settings.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden Home Directory File Write
dangerous_patterns.description: The logging example appends hook data to a file under ~/.claude. Hidden home directory writes can surprise users if paths and retention are not documented.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line directly appends to ~/.claude/file_log.txt. The surrounding context indicates audit logging rather than malicious concealment.
dangerous_patterns.remediation: Prefer explicit user-approved log paths and avoid logging sensitive tool input or output.

content.value_statement: Claude Code hook setup can be hard to structure and review safely. This skill provides hook patterns for validation, logging, notifications, and automation.
actual_capabilities: Explains Claude Code hook events and their intended use cases.
Provides a reusable hook configuration structure for settings.json.
Shows examples for PreToolUse validation and PostToolUse logging.
Shows a notification hook pattern for local desktop alerts.
Documents matcher patterns for tools, Bash commands, and MCP tools.
Provides a checklist for reviewing hook behavior before deployment.
limitations: It does not install hooks or modify settings.json by itself.
It does not validate generated shell commands for every platform.
It only documents basic hook patterns and examples.
Users must review privacy and security impact before enabling hooks.

use_cases.title: Block risky tool actions
use_cases.description: Create PreToolUse hooks that inspect tool activity before it runs.

use_cases.title: Record local file changes
use_cases.description: Design PostToolUse hooks that keep concise local audit records.

use_cases.title: Add workflow notifications
use_cases.description: Configure local notifications when Claude Code sends selected events.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic hook
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one event type.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a simple Claude Code hook for one event. Explain the matcher, command, and where it belongs.

prompt_templates.title: Add file write logging
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a PostToolUse hook that logs file write activity. Keep the command short and explain privacy considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Review a hook for safety
prompt_templates.scenario: Security review before adding a hook.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Claude Code hook configuration for security risks, unsafe shell quoting, noisy output, and broad matchers.

prompt_templates.title: Build a hook policy set
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a small Claude Code hook policy for validation, logging, and notifications. Include risks, test steps, and rollback guidance.

output_examples.input: I need a hook that records file write events.
output_examples.output: A PostToolUse logging design with a specific Write matcher, a concise local log target, and privacy review notes.

output_examples.input: I want alerts when Claude Code needs attention.
output_examples.output: A notification hook plan with a local alert command, platform notes, and guidance to avoid leaking sensitive messages.

output_examples.input: Check whether my hook matcher is too broad.
output_examples.output: A review that identifies broad matchers, suggests narrower patterns, and lists tests before enabling the hook.

best_practices: Review every hook command before adding it to Claude Code settings.
Use the narrowest matcher that still covers the intended workflow.
Keep hook output concise and avoid logging sensitive tool input.
anti_patterns: Do not use broad matchers for commands that change files or system state.
Do not log full tool inputs or outputs without checking for secrets.
Do not run slow hook commands that block normal Claude Code work.

faq.question: Does this skill install hooks automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users must add any hook configuration themselves.

faq.question: Which Claude Code hook events does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Notification, and Stop events.

faq.question: Can it help with security controls?
faq.answer: Yes. It can help design validation hooks, but users must verify command safety.

faq.question: Does it support Codex users?
faq.answer: Yes. Codex can use the skill content to explain and review Claude Code hook configurations.

faq.question: Can hooks access local files?
faq.answer: Hook commands can interact with local files if the user configures them to do so.

faq.question: What is the main safety concern?
faq.answer: Hooks can run shell commands automatically, so users should review commands and matchers carefully.
